images.caption:
Figure 1:Overview ofWorldMark.
Current interactive world models each define their own control interfaces, scenes, and evaluation protocols, making cross-model comparison impossible.WorldMarkresolves this fragmentation through three pillars:(a)A curated test suite of 500 cases spanning first- and third-person views with both photorealistic and stylized reference scenes, ensuring broad coverage of real-world evaluation conditions.(b)A unified action-mapping layer that translates a shared action vocabulary—WASD for movement and L/R for yaw rotation—into each model’s native control format (caption prompts, pose parameters, gamepad signals, action vectors, etc.), so that all models receive semantically identical instructions.(c)Under these standard conditions, feeding thesamereference image andsameaction sequence to different models reveals striking quality divergence that proprietary benchmarks obscure.
Figure 2:Overview of the Image Suite. Diverse scenes and styles are covered, each shown in first-person and generated third-person views.
Figure 3:The 15 standardized action sequences, ranging from elementary translations and rotations to combined and cyclic trajectories.
Figure 4:Context-aware action selection. A VLM analyzes the initial image to identify physical constraints and selects plausible action sequences from the predefined library.

Figure 5:Qualitative comparison across three evaluation dimensions. The top row demonstrates successful video generation (✓), while the bottom row illustrates common failures (✗) in Visual Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistency.

Figure 6:Spearman correlation between human preference rankings and automated metric scores across three World Consistency dimensions. Each point represents an evaluated model. The highρ\rhovalues demonstrate a strong positive correlation, indicating our automated metrics closely align with human perceptual judgments.
